content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To     : GOVERNMENT CODE SECTION 86202                    Date : Dec. 20, 1978


From   : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Barbara Campbell

Subject: Advice concerning Congressman-Elect Fazio

         Lance Olson called yesterday to ask about the following
situation:

         Vic Fazio, after being elected to Congress
on November 7, 1978, resigned his Assembly office.
After Fazio had resigned his Assembly office, he
received a $1,000 contribution from a lobbyist.
Fazio wants to use the $1,000 to retire his
1978 General Election debts.

Lance asked if Fazio could accept the $1,000 contribution from
a lobbyist without violating Government Code Section 86202.

When Fazio resigned his office as an Assemblyman, he was
no longer an elected state officer within the meaning of Gov-
ernment Code Section 86202. Therefore the lobbyist was not
making a contribution to a state candidate or an elected state
officer.

After discussing the matter with other staff members, I
called Lance back to tell him Fazio could accept the $1,000
contribution.

BC:plh

sections.question: Lance asked if Fazio could accept the $1,000 contribution from a lobbyist without violating Government Code Section 86202.
sections.conclusion: After discussing the matter with other staff members, I called Lance back to tell him Fazio could accept the $1,000 contribution.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a former assemblyman who has resigned his office accept a $1,000 contribution from a lobbyist to retire his election debts without violating Government Code Section 86202?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, Fazio can accept the contribution because upon resigning his Assembly office, he was no longer an elected state officer within the meaning of Government Code Section 86200, and therefore the lobbyist was not making a contribution to a state candidate or elected state officer.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal memorandum format without traditional section headers. Question and conclusion extracted from body text. Clear advice provided by FPPC staff member Barbara Campbell. Document references Government Code Section 86202 and 86200.

QUESTION: Lance asked if Fazio could accept the $1,000 contribution from a lobbyist without violating Government Code Section 86202.

CONCLUSION: After discussing the matter with other staff members, I called Lance back to tell him Fazio could accept the $1,000 contribution.
embedding.first_500_words: **State of California** **Memorandum** To: Government Code Section 86202 Date: Dec. 20, 1978 From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Barbara Campbell Subject: Advice concerning Congressman-Elect Fazio Lance Olson called yesterday to ask about the following situation: Vic Fazio, after being elected to Congress on November 7, 1978, resigned his Assembly office. After Fazio had resigned his Assembly office, he received a $1,000 contribution from a lobbyist. Fazio wants to use the $1,000 to retire his 1978 General Election debts. Lance asked if Fazio could accept the $1,000 contribution from a lobbyist without violating Government Code Section 86202. When Fazio resigned his office as an Assemblyman, he was no longer an elected state officer within the meaning of Government Code Section 86200. Therefore the lobbyist was not making a contribution to a state candidate or an elected state officer. After discussing the matter with other staff members, I called Lance back to tell him Fazio could accept the $1,000 contribution. BC:plh
embedding.summary: The FPPC advised that Congressman-Elect Vic Fazio could accept a $1,000 contribution from a lobbyist to retire his 1978 General Election debts after resigning from his Assembly position, as he was no longer an elected state officer at that time.
